FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes data from the U.S. ABCD Study at baseline (ages 9-10) with n=9,718 children from 21 U.S. recruitment sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using machine learning framework to identify factors associated with fluid cognitive functioning (FCF) in ABCD data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Child Development (2021).

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Composite labeled "screen media activity" is analyzed. Step 2: Methods state composite includes social network sites, text/instant messages, TV shows/movies, R-rated movies, videos (YouTube), and video games. Composite bundles TV and video games (non-phone/non-SM items) without separate breakout. NOT_MET.

notes: Paper fails FT-IC2 because composite screen media activity includes non-qualifying items (TV, video games) without separate modality breakout.

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes prospective cohort data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study with N=11,025 children ages 9-10 at baseline.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using logistic regression to estimate associations between baseline screen time and binge-eating disorder at one-year follow-up.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in International Journal of Eating Disorders (2021).

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 finds phone/SM-specific variables paired with individual-trait outcome. Results show: "each additional hour of social networking (aOR 1.62, 95% CI 1.18–2.22), texting (aOR 1.40, 95% CI 1.08–1.82)...was significantly associated with binge-eating disorder." Social networking and texting are phone/SM-specific. Binge-eating disorder (DSM-5 criteria) is individual-trait-level mental health outcome. MET.

FT_IC3.evidence: Logistic regression analysis reports adjusted odds ratios (aOR) with 95% confidence intervals for all screen time variables paired with binge-eating disorder outcome.

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple numeric results reported: total screen time aOR 1.11 (95% CI 1.05–1.18), social networking aOR 1.62 (95% CI 1.18–2.22), texting aOR 1.40 (95% CI 1.08–1.82).

FT_EC7_reason: Design is longitudinal (baseline to 1-year follow-up). Screen time is DV in every analysis. No SM as IV analysis anywhere in paper (all analyses use screen time as exposure predicting outcomes). No cross-sectional SM-as-DV analysis. All three EC7 conditions met.
notes: Paper meets all IC criteria but is excluded under FT-EC7 longitudinal SM-as-DV-only rule.

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes prospective cohort data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study with N=11,066 children ages 9-10 at baseline.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using multiple linear regression to estimate associations between baseline screen time behaviors and BMI percentile at 1-year follow-up.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Pediatric Obesity (2021).

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 finds phone/SM-specific variables paired with individual-trait outcome. Results show: "each additional hour of texting (B = 0.92, 95% CI 0.29–1.55), video chat (B = 0.72, 95% CI 0.09–1.36)...was significantly prospectively associated with higher BMI percentile." Texting and video chat are phone/SM-specific. BMI percentile is individual-trait-level physical health outcome. MET.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression analysis reports unstandardized regression coefficients (B) with 95% confidence intervals for screen time variables predicting BMI percentile at follow-up.

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple numeric results reported: total screen time B=0.22 (95% CI 0.10–0.34), texting B=0.92 (95% CI 0.29–1.55), video chat B=0.72 (95% CI 0.09–1.36).

FT_EC7_reason: Design is longitudinal but screen time is IV (exposure), not DV in all analyses. FT-EC7 does not apply when SM is IV.
notes: Paper meets all criteria. Includes as qualifies on FT-IC2 with phone/SM-specific variables (texting, video chat) paired with individual-trait outcome (BMI percentile).
